chapter_summary: This prompt window splices two distinct openings. (1) Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 1 (Arjuna-Vishada Yoga), verses 1-15, set on the field of Kurukshetra. Blind king Dhritarashtra asks his charioteer Sanjaya what his sons (the Kauravas) and the sons of Pandu (the Pandavas) did once gathered for war. Sanjaya narrates: seeing the Pandava army drawn up in battle-array by Drupada's son (Dhrishtadyumna), prince Duryodhana approaches his teacher Drona and reviews the great warriors on both sides. He names Pandava champions (Bhima, Arjuna, Yuyudhana/Satyaki, Virata, Drupada, Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, Kashiraja, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, Shaibya, Yudhamanyu, Uttamaujas, the sons of Subhadra and Draupadi), then his own (Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, Ashvatthama, Vikarna, Bhurishrava son of Somadatta). He calls his own force, guarded by Bhishma, 'unlimited/insufficient' and the enemy's, guarded by Bhima, 'sufficient', and orders all to protect Bhishma at every entry. Bhishma, the aged grandsire of the Kurus, roars like a lion and blows his conch; conches, drums and horns erupt into a tumultuous din. Then Krishna (Madhava/Hrishikesha) and Arjuna (Pandava/Dhananjaya), seated in a great chariot yoked to white horses, blow their divine conches — Krishna his Panchajanya, Arjuna his Devadatta, and Bhima (Vrikodara) of terrible deeds his great conch Paundra. (2) Gheranda Samhita, first upadesha tail (GhS_1.44-1.60) and the opening of the second upadesha (GhS_2.1-2.6), the dialogue of sage Gheranda and king Chanda(-kapali). The first block completes the shatkarma (six purificatory acts): basti (the two enemas, water jala-basti drawn in by ashvini-mudra while squatting navel-deep in water, and dry sthala/shushka-basti, curing prameha, gudavarta and gross wind), neti (passing a thin thread through the nostril, granting khechari-siddhi and divine sight), nauli/lauliki (churning the belly side to side to destroy all disease and kindle the digestive fire), trataka (steady gazing without blinking at a subtle point until tears flow, granting the shambhavi state and divine vision), and the three forms of kapalabhati (vatakrama via ida/pingala alternation, vyutkrama drawing water through the nose, and shitkrama drinking and expelling water) which remove kapha, banish old age and fever, and make one like Kamadeva. The second upadesha then opens the asana catalogue: of the 8,400,000 postures Shiva once spoke, 84 are choice and 32 are beneficial for mortals; it lists those thirty-two seats by name (Siddha, Padma, Bhadra, Mukta, Vajra, Svastika, Simha, Gomukha, Vira, Dhanura, Mrita, Gupta, Matsya, Matsyendra, Goraksha, Paschimottana, Utkata, Sankata, Mayura, Kukkuta, Kurma, Uttanakurmaka, Uttanamanduka, Vriksha, Manduka, Garuda, Vrisha, Shalabha, Makara, Ushtra, Bhujanga, Yoga), declaring them givers of siddhi in the mortal world, and begins describing Siddhasana.

story.title: Two Thresholds: The Conches of Kurukshetra and the Purification of the Vessel
story.arc: A blind king's question opens onto a battlefield where two armies face off; the prince inventories his champions, leans on his grandsire Bhishma, and the conches of both sides erupt — Bhishma's lion-roar against the divine Panchajanya and Devadatta of Krishna and Arjuna. The narrative halts at the held breath before war. The second thread is its inward mirror: sage Gheranda finishes teaching king Chanda the six cleansings that scour the body, then declares that of millions of postures only thirty-two matter for mortals, and begins seating the seeker for the long inner war.
story.timeline_note: Gita 1 is the literal opening of the Mahabharata war, just before Krishna's discourse. The Gheranda block is undated technical instruction (Hatha-Yoga manual), not narrative time; the GhS_1 tail closes the first upadesha and GhS_2.1-2.6 opens the second.
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana nervously bragging about his own army to his own teacher, then immediately ordering everyone to babysit grandpa Bhishma — overconfidence with a panic chaser. On the yoga side: the deadpan promise that doing enemas and threading string up your nose will make you 'like Kamadeva, the god of love.'

teachings.teaching: The battlefield is named dharmakshetra-kurukshetra — the 'field of dharma' — before a single weapon is raised, framing the coming war as a moral and inner contest, not merely a clash of armies.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga lens Kurukshetra is read inwardly: the body is the field, and the war is the seeker's own struggle between the descending pull of the senses (the Kaurava host) and the ascending aspiration guided by the indwelling Self. Krishna as charioteer is the awakened consciousness seated beside Arjuna the striving ego; the discourse begins only once the body-field is recognized as the true arena.

teachings.teaching: The divine conches of Krishna (Panchajanya) and Arjuna (Devadatta), seated together in one chariot drawn by white horses, sound after and against the conch of Bhishma, marking the side of the Self with its own irresistible sound.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the conch is nada, the inner sound heard when prana is gathered and turned upward; the white horses are the purified senses; the single chariot bearing both Krishna and Arjuna is the union of the lower self with the witnessing consciousness. The sounding of the divine conch is the inner declaration that the ascent has begun once the vehicle (body and senses) is yoked and steady.

teachings.teaching: Before any posture or breath, Gheranda prescribes the six purifications — the two bastis, neti, nauli/lauliki, trataka, and the three kapalabhatis — to clear bowel, sinus, belly-fire, eyes, and phlegm, declaring they remove disease, banish age and fever, and make one 'like Kamadeva.'
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches the body is the first instrument and the first obstacle; a vessel clogged with impurity cannot hold steady prana. The shatkarma is the literal scouring of the channels so the breath later runs clean through ida, pingala and sushumna. The 'destruction of disease' is the clearing of obstruction so the higher kriya has an unblocked road; health here is precondition, not goal.

teachings.teaching: Trataka — fixing the gaze without blinking on a single subtle point until tears fall — is said to produce the shambhavi state, prevent eye-disease, and grant divine sight.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's framework the steady, unblinking gaze is the first deliberate arrest of the moving mind: a fixed eye fixes the chitta. Trataka leading to the shambhavi mudra is attention turning from the outward object back toward its own source between the brows — the seed of the inward turn that the whole system is built to deepen. The 'divine sight' is the inner eye opening once the outer eye is stilled.

teachings.teaching: Kapalabhati is taught in three forms — vatakrama (alternating breath through ida and pingala), vyutkrama (drawing water up the nostrils), and shitkrama (drinking and expelling water through nose and mouth) — all to expel kapha and renew the body.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the alternation of ida and pingala as the deliberate balancing of the two solar/lunar currents so that prana can be coaxed into the central channel. Kapalabhati 'shining the skull' is the kindling of the inner fire that burns impurity from the nadis; the explicit nostril-and-water krama work belong to the direct Guru-disciple transmission and ripen only within that bond — the verse names the act, not the secret regulation of breath the act prepares.

teachings.teaching: Of 8,400,000 postures Shiva once spoke, 84 are choice and only 32 are beneficial for mortals; these thirty-two seats are declared givers of siddhi in the human world.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma honors that the asanas descend from Shiva, the Adi-Yogi — they are not invented but received through lineage. The radical narrowing from millions to thirty-two is the principle that the practice is not collection but selection: a few precise seats, built to hold the body sealed and still, are what actually consummate the inner work. The seat is scaffolding so the gaze and breath can do the real yoga.
